title: Fundamental Principles
overview: The United Kingdom is built on a set of shared values that guide how people live and work together. These fundamental principles support fairness, freedom, and respect for all. They are the foundation of British democracy and help ensure that everyone can take part in society safely and equally.

study_sections.heading: Five Core Values
study_sections.content: UK's core values are: Democracy (people choose government through free and fair elections), Rule of Law (no one—including government—is above law), Individual Liberty (right to make personal choices, speak freely, follow any faith or none), Equality (everyone treated fairly regardless of gender, race, religion, age, background), Mutual Respect and Tolerance (value others' beliefs and lifestyles even if disagree).

study_sections.heading: Promoting Values
study_sections.content: Principles promoted in schools and public institutions to build strong communities. British laws such as Human Rights Act (1998) and Equality Act (2010) protect these values. Freedom of religion and speech are key rights—balanced by duty to respect others.

study_sections.heading: Living the Values
study_sections.content: Freedom exists within law. Respect shown through listening and treating others fairly. Equality ensures everyone's vote and voice count equally. Democracy encourages participation. Shared values help diverse backgrounds live peacefully together.

key_facts: British society is founded on democracy, the rule of law, individual liberty and mutual respect and tolerance.
Free and fair elections allow people to choose representatives at national, devolved and local levels.
The rule of law ensures that laws apply equally to everyone and that justice is delivered by independent courts.
Individual liberty protects freedoms such as speech, thought, association and movement within the law.
Mutual respect and tolerance require people to value diversity and challenge discrimination and extremism.

The Equality Act 2010 protects characteristics including age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sex and sexual orientation.
Freedom of religion includes the right to follow no religion at all.
Responsibilities such as paying taxes, serving on juries and caring for family help uphold these values.
Schools, councils, police and courts are required to promote British values in their work.
These shared principles help communities live together peacefully and protect human rights.
memory_hook: Imagine British values as four interlocking gears—democracy, law, liberty and respect. When each turns, society runs smoothly; neglect one gear and the whole machine slows.

flashcards: What are the five fundamental British values?
Democracy, rule of law, individual liberty, mutual respect, tolerance
People choosing government in free elections
What does the rule of law mean?
Freedom to make choices and express views
Fair treatment and equal rights for all
Valuing others even when you disagree
Which law protects freedom of speech?
Which law protects people from discrimination?
Why are freedom and law linked?
Why is equality important in a democracy?
Everyone's vote and voice count equally
Why are these values important for the UK?
Help people live peacefully and fairly
